category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Linear Programming — Objective Function Optimization

questiontext.text: Maximize \(z=3x+5y\) subject to \(x+2y\le4\), \(3x+2y\le6\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). The maximum value of \(z\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(21/2\)

Corner points: \((0,0)\to0\), \((2,0)\to6\), \((1,\tfrac{3}{2})\to3+\tfrac{15}{2}=\tfrac{21}{2}\), \((0,2)\to10\). Maximum is \(\tfrac{21}{2}=10.5\) at \((1,\tfrac{3}{2})\). (Intersection: \(x+2y=4\) and \(3x+2y=6\) gives \(2x=2\Rightarrow x=1,y=3/2\).)

questiontext.text: The maximum value of \(z=x+y\) subject to \(|x|+|y|\le1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(1\)

The feasible region \(|x|+|y|\le1\) is the unit diamond with vertices \((\pm1,0)\), \((0,\pm1)\). The objective \(z=x+y\) is maximized on the edge from \((1,0)\) to \((0,1)\) where \(y=1-x\), giving \(z=1\) everywhere on this edge. Maximum is \(1\).

questiontext.text: Maximize \(z=2x_1+x_2\) subject to \(x_1+x_2\le4\), \(x_1-x_2\le2\), \(x_1\ge0\), \(x_2\ge0\). The maximum value of \(z\) is:

For a linear program: Maximise \(\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}\) subject to \(A\mathbf{x}\le\mathbf{b}\), \(\mathbf{x}\ge\mathbf{0}\), the dual is: Minimise \(\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{y}\) subject to \(A^T\mathbf{y}\ge\mathbf{c}\), \(\mathbf{y}\ge\mathbf{0}\). If both primal and dual are feasible, then at optimality:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Primal optimal value = Dual optimal value

The Strong Duality Theorem states that if both the primal and dual LP are feasible, then both attain optimal values and these values are equal: \(\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}^*=\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{y}^*\). Weak duality: \(\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}\le\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{y}\) for any feasible \(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\).

questiontext.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming states that if a linear program has an optimal solution, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) An optimal solution exists at a corner (extreme) point of the feasible region

Because the feasible region of an LP is a convex polyhedron and the objective function is linear, if the optimum is attained it is attained at a vertex (corner point / basic feasible solution). There may also be optimal solutions along an edge (infinitely many), but one corner will always be optimal.

answer.text: An optimal solution exists at a corner point of the feasible region
The optimal solution is always unique
The feasible region is always bounded
The objective must be non-negative at all corners

questiontext.text: The standard form of a linear program (for the simplex method) is: Maximize \(\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}\) subject to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b},\;\mathbf{x}\ge\mathbf{0}\)

Standard form converts all inequalities to equalities by introducing slack/surplus variables. \(\le\) constraints get a slack variable (\(+s\)), \(\ge\) constraints get a surplus variable (\(-s\)) plus artificial variables for phase-1. The canonical form is \(A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}\), \(\mathbf{x}\ge0\).

questiontext.text: Consider the LP: Maximize \(x+y\) subject to \(x+y\ge5\), \(x+y\le3\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). This LP is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Infeasible

The constraints \(x+y\ge5\) and \(x+y\le3\) are contradictory (no point satisfies both simultaneously). Thus the feasible region is empty and the LP is infeasible — no solution exists.

questiontext.text: Consider the LP: Maximize \(x+2y\) subject to \(-x+y\le2\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). This LP is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Unbounded

The feasible region is non-empty. Taking \(x=0, y\to\infty\): \(-0+y=y\le2\) is violated for large \(y\). But take \(x=t, y=t\) for large \(t\): \(-t+t=0\le2\) ✓ and \(z=t+2t=3t\to\infty\). The objective is unbounded above.

questiontext.text: For an LP in standard form with \(m\) equality constraints and \(n\) variables (\(n>m\)), a basic feasible solution (BFS) is a solution where:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) At most \(m\) variables are nonzero, corresponding to a basis of \(A\)

A basic solution sets \(n-m\) non-basic variables to 0 and solves for the remaining \(m\) basic variables. If all basic variables are \(\ge0\), it is a basic feasible solution. BFS ↔ corner points of the feasible polytope.

answer.text: At most \(m\) variables are nonzero, forming a basis
The objective function value is zero

questiontext.text: Complementary slackness conditions for primal-dual LP optimality state that at optimal solutions \(\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{y}^*\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(x_j^*(c_j-(A^Ty^*)_j)=0\) and \(y_i^*(b_i-(Ax^*)_i)=0\) for all \(i,j\)

Complementary slackness: for each primal variable \(x_j\) and its dual slack \(c_j-(A^Ty^*)_j\), at least one must be zero. Similarly for each dual variable \(y_i\) and primal slack \(b_i-(Ax^*)_i\). These conditions are both necessary and sufficient for optimality (with feasibility).

questiontext.text: The feasible region of a linear program (a set defined by finitely many linear inequalities) is always:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A convex polytope (possibly unbounded or empty)

Each linear inequality \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{x}\le b\) defines a half-space, which is convex. The intersection of finitely many convex sets is convex. Such a set is called a convex polytope (bounded) or polyhedron (possibly unbounded). It may be empty (infeasible LP).

answer.text: A convex polyhedron (possibly unbounded or empty)

questiontext.text: In an LP, the dual variable (shadow price) \(y_i^*\) corresponding to constraint \(i\) represents:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The rate of change of the optimal objective value with respect to the right-hand side \(b_i\)

By sensitivity analysis: if \(b_i\) increases by \(\Delta\), the optimal objective changes by approximately \(y_i^*\cdot\Delta\). This marginal value interpretation makes shadow prices useful in resource allocation problems.

answer.text: The value of constraint \(i\) at optimality
The slack variable value for constraint \(i\)
The rate of change of optimal objective w.r.t. \(b_i\)
The coefficient of \(x_i\) in the objective function

questiontext.text: Maximize \(z=4x+3y\) subject to \(|x|+|y|\le2\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). The maximum value is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(8\)

Feasible region (first quadrant part of \(|x|+|y|\le2\)): triangle with vertices \((0,0),(2,0),(0,2)\). Evaluate \(z\): at \((0,0)\to0\), \((2,0)\to8\), \((0,2)\to6\). Maximum \(z=8\) at \((2,0)\).

questiontext.text: A transportation problem has supply \([30,\,20]\), demand \([25,\,25]\), and cost matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}2&3\\4&1\end{pmatrix}\). The minimum transportation cost is:
The dual of: Maximize \(3x_1+2x_2\) subject to \(x_1+x_2\le4\), \(2x_1+x_2\le6\), \(x_1,x_2\ge0\) is: Minimize \(4y_1+6y_2\) subject to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(y_1+2y_2\ge3,\;y_1+y_2\ge2,\;y_1,y_2\ge0\)

For each primal variable \(x_j\ge0\), the dual constraint is \(A^T_j\mathbf{y}\ge c_j\). Column 1: \(y_1+2y_2\ge3\). Column 2: \(y_1+y_2\ge2\). Dual objective: minimise \(\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{y}=4y_1+6y_2\).

name.text: LP - Simplex Pivot Column (Most Negative Reduced Cost)

questiontext.text: In the simplex method (maximization), the entering variable is chosen as the non-basic variable with:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The most negative reduced cost

Reduced cost \(\bar{c}_j=c_j-c_B^TB^{-1}A_j\). A negative reduced cost means increasing \(x_j\) improves the objective. The standard rule chooses the most negative reduced cost (Dantzig's rule) to select the entering variable.

answer.text: The variable with the largest coefficient in \(\mathbf{c}\)

questiontext.text: Maximize \(z=5x+4y\) subject to \(6x+4y\le24\), \(x+2y\le6\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). The maximum value of \(z\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(21\)

Corner points: \((0,0)\to0\), \((4,0)\to20\), \((3,3/2)\)—from \(6x+4y=24\) and \(x+2y=6\): subtract to get \(5x=18\Rightarrow x=18/5\), hmm. Let me redo: \(6x+4y=24\) and \(x+2y=6\). Multiply second by 2: \(2x+4y=12\). Subtract from first: \(4x=12\Rightarrow x=3, y=3/2\). \(z=15+6=21\). Also \((0,3)\to12\). Maximum \(z=21\) at \((3,3/2)\).

questiontext.text: Minimize \(z=2x+3y\) subject to \(x+y\ge4\), \(x+3y\ge6\), \(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\). The minimum value of \(z\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(8\)

Corner points of feasible region: \((0,4): z=12\), \((3,1): z=9\), \((6,0): z=12\). Intersection of \(x+y=4\) and \(x+3y=6\): \(2y=2\Rightarrow y=1, x=3\). \(z=6+3=9\). Minimum is \(9\) at \((3,1)\). Actually let me recheck: \(z=2(3)+3(1)=6+3=9\). Check \((0,4)\): \(z=0+12=12\). Check \((6,0)\): constraints satisfied? \(6+0=6\ge4\)✓, \(6+0=6\ge6\)✓. \(z=12\). Minimum \(z=9\).

name.text: LP - Number of Basic Feasible Solutions Bound

questiontext.text: An LP in standard form with \(m\) equality constraints and \(n\) decision variables (\(n>m\)) has at most how many basic solutions?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\binom{n}{m}\)

A basic solution is determined by choosing \(m\) variables as basic (setting the other \(n-m\) to zero). There are \(\binom{n}{m}\) ways to choose the basis. Not all give feasible solutions (some may have negative basic variables). The simplex method visits only BFS, reducing the search from exponential to polynomial in practice.

name.text: LP - Max z=ax+by on unit square

questiontext.text: Let \(a,b>0\). The maximum of \(z=ax+by\) over the unit square \(\{(x,y):0\le x\le1,\,0\le y\le1\}\) is attained at:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \((1,1)\) with maximum \(a+b\)

Since \(a,b>0\), the objective increases as \(x\) and \(y\) increase. The maximum is attained at \((1,1)\) giving \(z=a+b\). The four corners give \(0, a, b, a+b\) at \((0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1)\) respectively.
